name: Differential Equations
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 9.1 Definitions: Order, Degree, Solutions
sections.body:
A differential equation (DE) is an equation involving an unknown function and its derivatives.

Order: order of the highest derivative present.

Degree: power of the highest-order derivative when the DE is polynomial in derivatives (after removing radicals/fractional powers). Degree may be undefined.

A general solution contains as many arbitrary constants as the order. A particular solution is obtained by giving these constants specific values.

Example: $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}+\sin\!\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)=0$ has order $2$ but degree undefined (not polynomial in $y'$).

sections.heading: 9.2 Forming a Differential Equation
sections.body:
Start from a family of curves $f(x,y,c_1,c_2,\dots,c_n)=0$ with $n$ arbitrary constants. Differentiate $n$ times w.r.t. $x$. Eliminate the $n$ constants among the $n+1$ relations. The result is a DE of order $n$.

Example: family $y=mx$ — one constant $m$. Differentiate: $y'=m$. Eliminate: $y=y'\,x$, i.e. $x\,\dfrac{dy}{dx}=y$.

sections.heading: 9.3 Variables Separable Method
sections.body:
A first-order DE $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=F(x,y)$ is separable if $F(x,y)=g(x)\,h(y)$. Then

$\dfrac{dy}{h(y)}=g(x)\,dx\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;\int\dfrac{dy}{h(y)}=\int g(x)\,dx+C.$

Example: $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=ky$ gives $\ln|y|=kx+C$, i.e. $y=Ae^{kx}$.

sections.heading: 9.4 Homogeneous DEs
sections.body:
A function $F(x,y)$ is homogeneous of degree $n$ if $F(\lambda x,\lambda y)=\lambda^{n}F(x,y)$. A first-order DE $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=F(x,y)$ is homogeneous if the RHS is homogeneous of degree $0$, i.e. $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=g\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right).$

Substitution. Put $y=vx$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=v+x\dfrac{dv}{dx}$. The DE becomes separable in $v,x$:

$v+x\dfrac{dv}{dx}=g(v)\;\Longrightarrow\;\dfrac{dv}{g(v)-v}=\dfrac{dx}{x}.$

sections.heading: 9.5 Linear First-Order DE
sections.body:
Standard form: $\dfrac{dy}{dx}+P(x)\,y=Q(x).$

Integrating factor (IF): $\mu(x)=e^{\int P(x)\,dx}.$ Multiplying both sides by $\mu$ makes the LHS the derivative of $\mu y$:

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\mu(x)\,y\right)=\mu(x)\,Q(x).$

Solution: $y\cdot\mu(x)=\int\mu(x)\,Q(x)\,dx+C.$

For $\dfrac{dx}{dy}+P(y)\,x=Q(y)$, swap roles: IF $=e^{\int P(y)\,dy}$, solution $x\cdot$IF$=\int Q\cdot$IF$\,dy+C$.

sections.heading: 9.6 Initial Value Problems & Applications
sections.body:
An initial value problem supplies a value $y(x_0)=y_0$ to determine the constant in the general solution.

Common applications:

  • Population/decay: $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=kN\Rightarrow N=N_0 e^{kt}$.
  • Newton's law of cooling: $\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-T_s)$.
  • Mixing tanks: rate in $-$ rate out, gives a linear DE in concentration.

summary: A differential equation involves derivatives of an unknown function. Identify order (highest derivative) and degree. Forming a DE: differentiate the family enough times to eliminate constants. Solving methods on the syllabus: (1) separation of variables; (2) homogeneous DE via $y=vx$ substitution; (3) linear first-order via integrating factor $e^{\int P\,dx}$. Initial conditions pin down constants.
pitfalls: Trying to separate a non-separable equation.
Substituting $y=vx$ when the DE is not homogeneous of degree 0.
Forgetting to multiply BOTH sides by the integrating factor.
Dropping the constant of integration before applying the initial condition.
Writing the IF as $e^{P}$ instead of $e^{\int P\,dx}$.
